title: Mamba: The Selective Memory Machine
subtitle: From control theory to selective state spaces — how Mamba achieves linear-time sequence modeling by learning what to remember and what to forget

children.children: Transformers are brilliant but expensive. Every token attends to every other token, which means the attention matrix scales as O(n²) in sequence length. Double the context window, quadruple the compute.

Attention cost for sequence length n:

  n = 1,000    →   1,000,000 operations     (manageable)
  n = 10,000   → 100,000,000 operations     (expensive)
  n = 100,000  → 10,000,000,000 operations  (brutal)
  n = 1,000,000 → 1,000,000,000,000 ops     (impossible)

Scaling: O(n²) — every token looks at every other token

But there's a deeper problem: transformers have no persistent memory. At every layer, every token re-computes its relationships with all other tokens from scratch. There's no compressed summary of 'what came before' — just the raw tokens and the attention matrix connecting them.

Transformer processing "The cat sat on the mat and then it..."

  To predict what comes after "it":
  → Compute attention from "it" to ALL 9 preceding tokens
  → Build a weighted sum of their value vectors
  → No shortcut. No summary. Must re-examine everything.

  Now add 10,000 more tokens of context:
  → Must attend to ALL 10,009 tokens
  → Even though most of them are irrelevant to "it"
  → The KV cache grows linearly, attention grows quadratically

props.analogy: Imagine reading a 500-page novel, but you have no memory. Every time you encounter a pronoun like 'she,' you have to flip back through ALL previous pages to figure out who 'she' refers to. You can't just remember 'the protagonist is Maria' — you must re-read every mention of every character, every time.
props.insight: This is exactly what transformers do. The attention mechanism is a lookup over the entire history. It works, but it's absurdly expensive for long sequences. What if you could just... remember?

children.children: What we want: a model that maintains a compressed, evolving summary of everything it's seen — a hidden state that gets updated with each new token, in O(1) per step. This is exactly what State Space Models do.

props.title: State Space Models: Memory as Hidden State

children.children: State Space Models (SSMs) come from control theory — the branch of engineering that models how systems evolve over time. The core idea: a hidden state vector h(t) summarizes everything relevant about the system's history, and it evolves according to simple linear equations.

The Continuous State Space Equations:

  dh/dt = A·h(t) + B·u(t)     ← State evolution
  y(t)  = C·h(t) + D·u(t)     ← Output

Where:
  h(t) ∈ ℝᴺ  = hidden state (compressed memory)
  u(t) ∈ ℝ    = input signal
  y(t) ∈ ℝ    = output signal
  A ∈ ℝᴺˣᴺ   = state transition matrix (how memory evolves)
  B ∈ ℝᴺˣ¹   = input projection (what to write into memory)
  C ∈ ℝ¹ˣᴺ   = output projection (what to read from memory)
  D ∈ ℝ       = skip connection (usually ignored)

The hidden state h is the key insight: it IS compressed memory. At any moment, h contains a fixed-size summary of the entire input history. The matrix A controls how this memory evolves — what fades, what persists, what interacts.

props.concept: SSM as a Conveyor Belt with Selective Memory
props.analogy: Think of the hidden state as a conveyor belt in a factory. Items (input tokens) arrive one at a time. The belt has fixed capacity — N slots. Matrix A controls how items shift and decay on the belt (old items fade). Matrix B decides which aspects of a new item get loaded onto the belt. Matrix C reads off the belt to produce output. The belt never gets longer — it's always N slots. Old information gets compressed, not stored verbatim.
props.insight: Unlike a transformer's attention (which is like having a warehouse of every item ever seen), the SSM conveyor belt maintains a fixed-size, continuously-updated summary. Processing the next item is always O(N), regardless of how many items came before.

children.children: Discretization: From Continuous to Digital

Neural networks process discrete sequences (token by token), not continuous signals. So we need to discretize the continuous SSM equations. This is done via a step size Δ (delta) that controls how much continuous time passes between discrete steps.

Discretization (Zero-Order Hold):

  Continuous:  dh/dt = A·h + B·u

  Discrete:    h[k] = Ā·h[k-1] + B̄·u[k]
               y[k] = C·h[k]

  Where:
    Ā = exp(Δ·A)           ← discretized state matrix
    B̄ = (Δ·A)⁻¹(Ā - I)·Δ·B  ← discretized input matrix
    Δ = step size            ← controls time resolution

  Think of Δ as a zoom level:
    Small Δ → fine-grained, high-resolution (captures fast changes)
    Large Δ → coarse, low-resolution (skips over details, sees big picture)

The step size Δ is crucial. It controls the trade-off between resolution and memory span. A small Δ means 'pay close attention to each token' while a large Δ means 'skip over details and focus on the long-range pattern.' In Mamba, Δ becomes input-dependent — the model learns WHEN to zoom in and when to skim.

props.title: State Evolution: Watch the Hidden State Compress History
props.prompt: Trace how the hidden state evolves as tokens arrive. Notice how it's always a fixed-size vector — old information gets compressed, not stored.

scenarios.input: Token 1: 'The' arrives → B maps it into the hidden state
scenarios.reveal: h[1] = Ā·h[0] + B̄·'The' = [0.0, 0.0] + [0.3, 0.1] = [0.3, 0.1]. The hidden state now contains a compressed representation of 'The'. The state has 2 dimensions — that's all the memory we get.

scenarios.input: Token 2: 'cat' arrives → old state decays, new info added
scenarios.reveal: h[2] = Ā·[0.3, 0.1] + B̄·'cat' = [0.27, 0.08] + [0.5, 0.4] = [0.77, 0.48]. Notice: the old state [0.3, 0.1] got multiplied by Ā (which shrinks it slightly — that's decay). New information about 'cat' got added. The state now encodes BOTH tokens in just 2 numbers.

h[3] = Ā·[0.77, 0.48] + B̄·'sat' = [0.69, 0.38] + [0.2, 0.6] = [0.89, 0.98]. Three tokens compressed into 2 numbers. We can't perfectly reconstruct the original tokens, but the state captures the RELEVANT information — subject identity, action, grammatical state.

scenarios.input: After 1000 tokens: state is STILL just 2 numbers
scenarios.reveal: h[1000] = [0.42, 0.73]. Same size as h[1]. The first token's information has been exponentially decayed through 999 applications of Ā — it's mostly gone, unless A was carefully designed to preserve it. This is the fundamental trade-off: fixed memory, lossy compression. The question is: what should A preserve?

props.title: The HiPPO Framework: Remembering History Optimally

children.children: The SSM framework is elegant, but there's a critical question: how do you initialize the A matrix? A random A matrix will forget everything after a few steps. A poorly chosen A will remember the wrong things. The HiPPO framework (High-order Polynomial Projection Operator) solves this by designing A to optimally approximate the history of inputs.
The Core Idea: Project History onto Polynomials

HiPPO says: at every time step, the hidden state should store the coefficients of a polynomial approximation of the input history. Specifically, it projects the input function onto a basis of Legendre polynomials.

HiPPO: Online Polynomial Approximation of History

At time t, we want h(t) to encode:
  u(s) ≈ Σᵢ hᵢ(t) · Pᵢ(s)    for s ∈ [0, t]

Where Pᵢ are Legendre polynomials (orthogonal basis)

The HiPPO-LegS (Legendre, Scaled) matrix:

  Aₙₖ = -(2n+1)^(1/2) · (2k+1)^(1/2)   if n > k
  Aₙₖ = -(n+1)                            if n = k
  Aₙₖ = 0                                 if n < k

This specific A matrix has a remarkable property:
→ The hidden state h(t) is always the BEST polynomial
  approximation of the entire input history u(0..t).
→ It automatically rescales as time passes, keeping
  recent inputs at high resolution and old inputs compressed.

Legendre polynomials form an orthogonal basis on [-1, 1]. They're the 'best' basis for polynomial approximation in the least-squares sense — each added polynomial captures the maximum possible variance in the signal not captured by previous ones.

With N Legendre coefficients, you get the optimal N-dimensional summary of the input history. The first coefficient captures the mean, the second captures the slope, the third captures curvature, and so on. Higher-order coefficients capture finer temporal details.

What the hidden state dimensions encode:

  h[0] = mean of input history       (constant term)
  h[1] = trend / slope               (linear term)
  h[2] = curvature / acceleration     (quadratic term)
  h[3] = rate of curvature change     (cubic term)
  ...
  h[N] = finest temporal detail       (N-th order variation)

With N=64 (typical), you get a remarkably faithful
summary of the entire input history in just 64 numbers.

The key insight: HiPPO doesn't just remember recent inputs. It maintains a multi-resolution summary — recent events at high resolution, distant events at lower resolution. This is exactly the memory structure you'd want for language: remember the gist of the paragraph, the structure of the sentence, and the exact identity of the last few tokens.

props.analogy: Imagine a sketch artist who must capture a parade of people walking past. They can't photograph everyone (that's a transformer storing every token). Instead, they maintain a SKETCH that gets continuously updated. Each new person's features are blended into the sketch. The sketch has a fixed number of pencil strokes (N dimensions). Recent people are drawn in detail; older ones become vague impressions. The Legendre basis is the artist's technique — it guarantees the best possible sketch given a limited number of strokes.
props.insight: HiPPO answers the question 'what should A remember?' with a mathematically optimal answer: maintain the best polynomial approximation of history. This turns an arbitrary design choice into a principled initialization.

children.children: HiPPO gave us the right A matrix. But naively computing h[k] = Ā·h[k-1] + B̄·u[k] step by step is sequential — no better than an RNN. The S4 paper (Gu, Goel, Ré, 2022) made SSMs competitive by solving two problems: (1) how to parameterize A for stability and efficiency, and (2) how to compute the entire sequence in parallel.
The Dual View: Recurrence vs. Convolution
Here's the key insight: unrolling the recurrence reveals that an SSM is secretly a convolution.

Recurrent view (sequential, good for inference):
  h[0] = B̄·u[0]
  h[1] = Ā·h[0] + B̄·u[1] = Ā·B̄·u[0] + B̄·u[1]
  h[2] = Ā·h[1] + B̄·u[2] = Ā²·B̄·u[0] + Ā·B̄·u[1] + B̄·u[2]

Output:
  y[k] = C·h[k] = C·Āᵏ·B̄·u[0] + C·Āᵏ⁻¹·B̄·u[1] + ... + C·B̄·u[k]

Define the SSM kernel:
  K = (C·B̄, C·Ā·B̄, C·Ā²·B̄, ..., C·Āᴸ⁻¹·B̄)

Then: y = K * u  (convolution!)

Convolutional view (parallel, good for training):
  → Compute K once (the 'SSM kernel')
  → Apply it via FFT-based convolution: O(L log L)
  → Entire sequence processed in parallel!

This dual view is the breakthrough: during TRAINING, use the convolutional form for parallelism (like a transformer). During INFERENCE, use the recurrent form for O(1) per-step generation (like an RNN). Best of both worlds.
Diagonal Plus Low-Rank Structure

Computing the kernel K requires computing powers of Ā (Ā, Ā², Ā³, ...), which is expensive for a dense N×N matrix. S4's trick: decompose A into diagonal + low-rank form, which makes the kernel computable in O(N log N) via a connection to Cauchy kernels.
S4 uses the fact that the HiPPO matrix A can be decomposed as:

A = Λ + P·Qᵀ

Where:
  Λ = diagonal matrix (N eigenvalues)
  P, Q = low-rank correction (rank 1 or 2)

This makes the kernel computation:
  K̂(ω) = C·(iω - Λ - PQᵀ)⁻¹·B

Which, via the Woodbury identity, becomes:
  K̂(ω) = [Cauchy kernel] + [rank-1 correction]

Cauchy kernel: Σᵢ Cᵢ·Bᵢ / (iω - Λᵢ)
→ Computable in O(N) per frequency
→ Total: O(N·L) or O(N·L·log L) with FFT

Later simplifications (S4D, DSS) showed that simply using a diagonal A matrix works nearly as well, reducing the computation even further. The diagonal structure means each hidden state dimension evolves independently — a massive simplification that barely hurts performance.

S4 proved that SSMs could match transformers on the Long Range Arena benchmark — sequences of length 1,000 to 16,000 where transformers struggle. But there was still a problem: the A, B, C matrices were static. They didn't change based on the input. The model couldn't CHOOSE what to remember.

Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2023) introduces the key innovation that transforms SSMs from a niche architecture into a genuine transformer competitor: SELECTIVITY. The parameters B, C, and Δ become functions of the input, allowing the model to dynamically control what enters the state, what leaves it, and at what resolution.

Standard SSM (S4):
  h[k] = Ā·h[k-1] + B̄·u[k]     ← A, B, C are FIXED
  y[k] = C·h[k]                   ← Same filtering for every input

Mamba (Selective SSM):
  B[k] = Linear(u[k])             ← B depends on current input
  C[k] = Linear(u[k])             ← C depends on current input  
  Δ[k] = softplus(Linear(u[k]))   ← Step size depends on input
  
  Ā[k] = exp(Δ[k]·A)             ← Discretization now varies per step!
  B̄[k] = Δ[k]·B[k]              ← Input gate varies per step!
  
  h[k] = Ā[k]·h[k-1] + B̄[k]·u[k]   ← SELECTIVE state update
  y[k] = C[k]·h[k]                    ← SELECTIVE readout
Why Selectivity Changes Everything

Without selectivity, the SSM applies the same transformation to every token — it's a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system. It can't distinguish between important and unimportant tokens. With selectivity, Mamba becomes a data-dependent filter that can:
REMEMBER: When an important token arrives, make Δ large → B̄ is large → the token gets strongly written into the state
FORGET: When the model needs to reset, make Δ large → Ā approaches 0 → old state decays rapidly
IGNORE: When a filler token arrives, make Δ small → B̄ is small → the token barely affects the state
FOCUS: C[k] controls which aspects of the state are read out, letting the model attend to different stored information at different positions

props.title: Selective Filtering: Watch Mamba Choose What to Remember
props.prompt: See how input-dependent Δ lets Mamba selectively process a sequence. Important content tokens get large Δ (written to memory); filler tokens get small Δ (ignored).

scenarios.input: Input: 'The [IMPORTANT] capital of France is [IMPORTANT] Paris [FILLER] , which is [FILLER] also known as ...'
scenarios.reveal:
For 'capital': Δ = 0.9 (large) → strongly written to state. The model recognizes this as a key content word.
For 'of': Δ = 0.1 (small) → barely touches state. Grammatical filler.
For 'France': Δ = 0.85 → written to state. Key entity.
For 'is': Δ = 0.05 → ignored. Copula.
For 'Paris': Δ = 0.95 → STRONGLY written. The answer.
For ',': Δ = 0.01 → completely ignored.
For 'which': Δ = 0.08 → ignored. Relative pronoun doesn't carry content.

scenarios.input: Comparison: what would a non-selective SSM do?
scenarios.reveal: A fixed Δ (say 0.5) applies to EVERY token equally. 'The' gets as much state-writing power as 'Paris'. The model can't prioritize content over filler. It wastes its limited state capacity on commas and articles. This is why non-selective SSMs fail at content-based tasks — they can't do the selective copying that language requires.

Δ controls a trade-off:
• Large Δ → Ā = exp(Δ·A) → eigenvalues decay more → OLD state forgotten faster, BUT B̄ = Δ·B is large → NEW input written strongly.
• Small Δ → Ā ≈ I (identity) → state preserved as-is, B̄ ≈ 0 → new input ignored.

This is exactly like an LSTM gate! Δ is simultaneously the forget gate AND the input gate, but it's derived from a continuous-time ODE rather than being an ad hoc sigmoid.

children.children: The Hardware-Aware Algorithm
Making B, C, Δ input-dependent breaks the convolutional view — you can no longer precompute a single kernel K for the whole sequence. Mamba solves this with a hardware-aware parallel scan algorithm.

The Parallel Scan:

Naive recurrence (sequential, O(L) serial steps):
  h[1] = Ā[1]·h[0] + B̄[1]·u[1]
  h[2] = Ā[2]·h[1] + B̄[2]·u[2]
  h[3] = Ā[3]·h[2] + B̄[3]·u[3]
  ...must wait for h[k-1] before computing h[k]

Parallel scan (O(log L) serial steps):
  Step 1: Compute pairs (h[1],h[2]), (h[3],h[4]), ...
  Step 2: Combine pairs into quads
  Step 3: Combine quads into octets
  ...log₂(L) steps total

Key insight: the recurrence h[k] = a[k]·h[k-1] + b[k]
is an ASSOCIATIVE operation → parallel prefix scan applies!

Result: Training is O(L) work in O(log L) serial depth.
Inference is O(1) per step (just the recurrence).

Mamba's scan is carefully optimized for GPU memory hierarchy. The matrices Ā, B̄ are computed in SRAM (fast), the scan runs without materializing the full state sequence in HBM (slow). This hardware awareness is why Mamba is 3-5× faster than transformers at inference, not just theoretically but in practice.

props.concept: Mamba as a Smart Audio Mixer
props.analogy: Think of Mamba as a sound engineer at a live concert. The hidden state is the master recording. Each instrument (token) plays into the mixer. A static SSM would record every instrument at the same volume — drums, vocals, and crowd noise all equal. Mamba is a SMART mixer: it hears the content (input-dependent B, C, Δ) and dynamically adjusts the faders. When the vocalist sings (important content), crank the volume (large Δ). When the crowd murmurs (filler tokens), mute it (small Δ). The master recording (hidden state) captures what matters.
props.insight: Selectivity is what turns a generic signal processor into an intelligent content filter. The same mechanism that lets Mamba filter tokens is what lets LSTM gates work — but Mamba derives it from continuous-time dynamics, making it theoretically grounded and computationally efficient.

children.children: Mamba and transformers represent fundamentally different philosophies about how to process sequences. Neither is strictly better — they have complementary strengths.

TRANSFORMER              MAMBA
                    ───────────              ─────
Attention cost:     O(n²)                    O(n) — linear!
Memory per step:    O(n) KV cache            O(1) hidden state
Inference speed:    Slows with context       Constant per token
Parallelism:        Fully parallel           Parallel scan (training)
                                             Sequential (inference)

Context window:     Fixed (but large)        Theoretically unlimited
In-context learn:   Excellent                Weaker
Retrieval:          Excellent (direct attn)  Harder (must be in state)
Long-range deps:    O(1) via attention       Through state evolution

KV Cache:           Yes (grows with n)       None!
Generation speed:   ~30-50 tok/s (70B)       ~5× faster
Where Mamba Wins
Long sequences: DNA modeling (sequences of millions), audio processing, time series. Linear scaling means you can process sequences that would be impossible for transformers.
Streaming / real-time: Constant-time per-step inference. No growing KV cache. Perfect for real-time applications where you need to process tokens as they arrive.
Memory efficiency: No KV cache means much lower memory usage during inference. A Mamba model can serve more users on the same hardware.
Throughput: 3-5× higher generation throughput than equivalent-size transformers, because each step is O(1) not O(n).
Where Transformers Still Win

In-context learning: Transformers can look up any token in the context directly via attention. Mamba must have encoded it in the state — if the state wasn't designed to remember that specific information, it's lost.
Exact retrieval: 'What was the 5th word in the input?' is trivial for attention (just look at position 5) but extremely hard for an SSM (position 5 has been compressed into the state).

Few-shot prompting: The ability to condition on arbitrary in-context examples relies on attention's direct access to the context. SSMs struggle here because the examples must be compressed into a fixed-size state.

props.title: Attention vs. State: Two Ways to Remember
props.prompt: Compare how a transformer and Mamba handle the same retrieval task. Notice the fundamental architectural difference.

scenarios.input: Task: After reading 10,000 tokens, answer 'What was the name mentioned in token 42?'
scenarios.reveal:
TRANSFORMER: Easy. Attention reaches back to position 42, reads the value vector, done. Cost: O(1) lookup in the KV cache. The information was stored verbatim.

MAMBA: Difficult. Token 42 was processed 9,958 steps ago. Its information was written into the hidden state, then overwritten/compressed by 9,958 subsequent updates. Unless the model learned to permanently store names (unlikely for one mentioned once), the information is probably gone.

scenarios.input: Task: Summarize the themes of a 100,000-token document
scenarios.reveal:
TRANSFORMER: Struggles. 100K tokens means a 100K×100K attention matrix (10 billion entries). Even with flash attention, this is extremely expensive. The KV cache alone requires ~20GB for a 7B model.

MAMBA: Natural. The hidden state has been continuously updated throughout the document. Themes that recur get reinforced; details fade. The state IS a summary. Cost: same as processing 1,000 tokens — O(n) total.

scenarios.input: Task: Real-time transcription of a 3-hour meeting
scenarios.reveal:
TRANSFORMER: The KV cache grows continuously. After 2 hours, generation slows to a crawl as each new token must attend to hundreds of thousands of cached tokens. Eventually hits memory limits.

MAMBA: Constant speed throughout. Token 1 and token 500,000 are processed in exactly the same time. The hidden state size never changes. This is Mamba's killer application.

children.children: Mamba-2 (Dao & Gu, 2024) revealed a deep connection between SSMs and attention through the Structured State Space Duality (SSD) framework. The punchline: SSMs and attention are two views of the SAME computation.
The State Space Duality (SSD)

The Duality:

  SSM view:  h[k] = Ā[k]·h[k-1] + B̄[k]·u[k]
             y[k] = C[k]·h[k]

  Attention view:  y = M · (B·u)
  
  Where M is a STRUCTURED attention matrix:
    Mᵢⱼ = Cᵢ · (Āᵢ · Āᵢ₋₁ · ... · Āⱼ₊₁) · Bⱼ   for i ≥ j
    Mᵢⱼ = 0                                          for i < j (causal)

  Key insight: The SSM computes y = M·x where M is a
  SEMISEPARABLE matrix (low-rank below the diagonal).
  
  Standard attention: M = softmax(Q·Kᵀ) — dense.
  SSM attention: M = structured (rank N per block) — sparse.
  
  Same operation, different structure constraints on M!
This duality means you can implement Mamba-2 using matrix multiplications (like attention) rather than sequential scans, unlocking better hardware utilization on modern GPUs with tensor cores.
Mamba-1 used a parallel scan, which is work-efficient but not great at utilizing tensor cores (which want large matrix multiplies). Mamba-2 rewrites the computation as block matrix multiplications:

Mamba-2 Block Decomposition:

Divide sequence into chunks of size Q (e.g., Q=256)

Within each chunk:
  → Compute the structured attention matrix M (Q×Q)
  → Apply as matrix multiply: y_chunk = M · x_chunk
  → This uses tensor cores efficiently!

Between chunks:
  → Pass hidden state h from chunk to chunk (recurrent)
  → This is just a small matrix multiply

Result: 2-8× faster than Mamba-1 on modern GPUs

Mamba-2 also expands the state dimension significantly (from N=16 to N=64-256) because the matrix-multiply formulation handles larger states efficiently. Larger state = better memory = better performance.
Hybrid Architectures: Best of Both Worlds
The most exciting development: mixing SSM layers with attention layers in a single model. Each architecture handles what it's best at.

Hybrid Architectures:

Jamba (AI21, 2024):
  → 80% Mamba layers + 20% attention layers + MoE
  → 52B total params, 12B active (MoE)
  → Handles 256K context with far less memory than pure transformer
  → Attention layers handle retrieval; Mamba layers handle flow

Zamba (Zyphra, 2024):
  → Mamba backbone with shared attention layer
  → One attention layer, reused at multiple depths
  → 7B params, competitive with much larger models
  → Key insight: you don't need attention at EVERY layer

The pattern: use Mamba for most of the computation
(cheap, handles long context) and sprinkle in a few
attention layers for tasks that need exact retrieval.

The hybrid insight matches intuition: most of language processing is about maintaining and updating a running state (what SSMs do well). Only occasionally do you need to look up a specific fact from earlier in the context (what attention does well). Spending 80% of your compute on state maintenance and 20% on retrieval makes more sense than 100% on attention.

props.title: Why This Matters for Dendritic Diffusion

children.children: Here's where SSMs connect to something bigger. In dendritic diffusion, text generation branches like a crystal — a main trunk spawns sub-branches, which spawn their own sub-branches. Each branch needs CONTEXT from its parent. With transformers, every branch must attend to the entire document. With SSMs, something much more elegant is possible.
The Key Insight: Forking the Hidden State

Dendritic Diffusion with Transformers:

  Main trunk: [token₁, token₂, ..., token₁₀₀₀]
  Branch A (from position 500): must attend to tokens 1-500
  Branch B (from position 800): must attend to tokens 1-800
  Sub-branch A1 (from A, position 200): must attend to tokens 1-700

  Every branch carries the FULL context via KV cache.
  Cost: O(total_tokens × branch_length) per branch
  Memory: KV cache duplicated for every branch


Dendritic Diffusion with SSMs:

  Main trunk processes to position 500 → hidden state h₅₀₀
  Branch A: FORK h₅₀₀ → h_A₀ = copy(h₅₀₀)
  Branch A evolves: h_A₁, h_A₂, ... (independent)
  
  Main trunk continues to position 800 → h₈₀₀
  Branch B: FORK h₈₀₀ → h_B₀ = copy(h₈₀₀)
  Branch B evolves: h_B₁, h_B₂, ... (independent)

  Cost per branch: O(branch_length) only!
  Memory per fork: just copy the state vector (tiny!)
  No KV cache. No re-processing parent context.

This is the critical architectural advantage: an SSM hidden state IS the compressed context. Forking it is trivial — just copy the vector. Each branch inherits everything the parent knew, then evolves independently. The cost is proportional to the branch length, not the total document length.

props.concept: SSM State as a Plant's Meristem
props.analogy: In a growing plant, each meristem (growth tip) carries the biological 'state' — hormones, gene expression levels, nutrient concentrations — that determines how it grows. When a branch point forms, the meristem DIVIDES. Each daughter meristem inherits the parent's state and then evolves independently based on its local environment. The meristem doesn't need to re-read the entire plant's history — its current state IS its history, compressed.
props.insight: The SSM hidden state IS the meristem's state. Forking a branch in dendritic diffusion IS cell division. Each branch carries its growth context — the compressed summary of everything that came before — as a tiny vector, not a massive KV cache. This makes branching generation architecturally natural.

children.children: Consider a dendritic diffusion tree with branching factor b and depth d:

Branching SSM for Dendritic Diffusion:

Tree structure:
  Root → Branch₁, Branch₂, ..., Branchᵦ
  Each Branchᵢ → Sub₁, Sub₂, ..., Subᵦ
  Total branches: O(bᵈ)

At each branch point (position p on parent):
  h_child₀ = h_parent[p]              ← FORK: copy parent state
  h_child[k] = Ā[k]·h_child[k-1] + B̄[k]·u_child[k]  ← evolve

Cost analysis:
  Transformer:  O(bᵈ × L²) — each branch attends to full context
  SSM:          O(bᵈ × l)  — each branch only processes its own length l
               where l << L (branch length << total document)

Memory analysis:
  Transformer:  O(bᵈ × L × d_model) — KV cache per branch
  SSM:          O(bᵈ × N)            — just the state vector per branch
               where N ≈ 64, d_model ≈ 4096, L ≈ 10000
               → SSM uses ~600× less memory per branch!

With selective SSMs (Mamba), each branch can additionally adapt its filtering to the local content. A branch generating a factual paragraph might use large Δ for entity names and small Δ for connective tissue. A branch generating dialogue might prioritize speaker identity and emotional state. The selectivity lets each branch develop its own 'personality' in how it processes information.

The deepest insight: in a biological tree (real dendritic growth), and in a dendritic diffusion tree (text generation), and in a branching SSM — the SAME principle holds. Each growth point carries compressed context from its ancestry. The context evolves as the branch grows. Branching is cheap because you inherit the state, not the history. SSMs don't just model sequences — they model GROWTH.
A complete reference for the mathematical progression from classical SSMs to Mamba.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 EQUATION REFERENCE: From Control Theory to Mamba
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

1. CONTINUOUS STATE SPACE MODEL
   dh/dt = A·h(t) + B·u(t)
   y(t)  = C·h(t) + D·u(t)

2. DISCRETIZATION (Zero-Order Hold)
   Ā = exp(Δ·A)
   B̄ = (Δ·A)⁻¹(Ā - I)·Δ·B  ≈ Δ·B  (simplified)
   h[k] = Ā·h[k-1] + B̄·u[k]
   y[k] = C·h[k]

3. HIPPO INITIALIZATION (LegS variant)
   Aₙₖ = -(2n+1)^½·(2k+1)^½    (n > k)
   Aₙₖ = -(n+1)                  (n = k)
   Bₙ  = (2n+1)^½

4. S4 KERNEL (Convolutional View)
   K = (C·B̄, C·Ā·B̄, C·Ā²·B̄, ..., C·Āᴸ⁻¹·B̄)
   y = K * u    (FFT convolution, O(L log L))

5. MAMBA SELECTIVE PARAMETERS
   B[k] = W_B · u[k]            (input-dependent)
   C[k] = W_C · u[k]            (input-dependent)
   Δ[k] = softplus(W_Δ · u[k])  (input-dependent step size)

6. MAMBA-2 SSD (Structured Attention View)
   Mᵢⱼ = Cᵢ·(∏ₜ₌ⱼ₊₁ⁱ Āₜ)·Bⱼ  (structured semiseparable)
   y = M · (B·u)                 (block matrix multiply)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Transformers scale O(n²) and have no persistent memory. Every token re-examines the entire context. This works but is wasteful for long sequences.
State Space Models maintain a fixed-size hidden state that compresses history. Processing each new token is O(1). The state IS the memory.
HiPPO initializes the state transition matrix A to maintain an optimal polynomial approximation of history — solving the 'what to remember' problem with mathematics.
S4 showed SSMs can be computed as convolutions (parallel training) or recurrences (fast inference) — the dual view that made them competitive.
Mamba's selectivity (input-dependent B, C, Δ) lets the model CHOOSE what to remember and forget, turning a static filter into an intelligent content gate.
Mamba-2's SSD framework reveals that SSMs and attention are dual — the same computation with different structural constraints on the attention matrix.
Hybrid architectures (Jamba, Zamba) combine SSM layers for efficient state tracking with sparse attention layers for retrieval — likely the future of large language models.
For dendritic diffusion, SSMs are architecturally ideal: forking the hidden state at branch points gives each branch compressed context at near-zero cost, making tree-structured generation natural.

questions.question: What is the fundamental scaling difference between transformer attention and Mamba?
options: Transformers are O(n log n), Mamba is O(n)
Transformers are O(n²) in sequence length, Mamba is O(n)
Both are O(n²) but Mamba has a smaller constant
Transformers are O(n³), Mamba is O(n²)
questions.explanation: Transformer self-attention computes pairwise interactions between all n tokens, giving O(n²) cost. Mamba processes each token in O(1) by updating a fixed-size hidden state, giving O(n) total cost. This is the core architectural advantage.

questions.question: What does the HiPPO framework do?
options: It trains the model using hippocampus-inspired learning rules
It initializes the A matrix so the hidden state optimally approximates input history using polynomial projections
It prunes unimportant attention heads
It compresses the KV cache using polynomial approximation
questions.explanation: HiPPO (High-order Polynomial Projection Operator) designs the state transition matrix A so that at every time step, the hidden state stores the coefficients of the best polynomial approximation of the input history. This gives SSMs a principled initialization that preserves long-range information.

questions.question: What is the key innovation that distinguishes Mamba from S4?
options: Mamba uses a larger hidden state
Mamba makes B, C, and Δ input-dependent (selective), allowing data-dependent filtering
Mamba uses attention instead of convolutions
Mamba trains faster due to better optimization
questions.explanation: S4 uses fixed (input-independent) B, C, Δ parameters — it applies the same filter to every token. Mamba makes these parameters functions of the input, letting the model dynamically decide what to write into the state (B), what to read out (C), and at what resolution (Δ). This selectivity is what transforms SSMs from static filters into intelligent content-aware processors.

questions.question: In the SSM discretization, what does a large step size Δ do?
options: It makes the model slower
It increases the hidden state dimension
It causes faster decay of old state AND stronger writing of new input
It makes the output smoother
questions.explanation: A large Δ has two effects: (1) Ā = exp(Δ·A) has faster-decaying eigenvalues, so old state information fades quicker, and (2) B̄ ≈ Δ·B is larger, so the new input is written more strongly into the state. Mamba's key insight is making Δ input-dependent, so the model can choose when to reset (large Δ on important tokens) and when to coast (small Δ on filler).

questions.question: Why are SSMs particularly well-suited for dendritic (branching) text generation?
options: SSMs generate text faster than transformers
SSMs can process longer sequences
The hidden state can be cheaply forked at branch points — each branch inherits compressed context without duplicating the full KV cache
SSMs produce more creative text
questions.explanation: In dendritic diffusion, each branch needs context from its parent. With transformers, this requires duplicating the entire KV cache (O(n × d) per branch). With SSMs, you just copy the hidden state vector (O(N) per branch, where N ≈ 64). This is ~600× less memory per branch, and each branch's cost is proportional to its own length, not the total document length.

questions.question: What did Mamba-2's Structured State Space Duality (SSD) reveal?
options: That SSMs are always better than attention
That SSMs and attention are dual — the same computation with different structural constraints on the interaction matrix
That convolutions can replace both SSMs and attention
That the HiPPO matrix is unnecessary
questions.explanation: SSD showed that an SSM computes y = M·x where M is a structured (semiseparable) matrix, while attention computes the same operation with an unstructured (dense) matrix M = softmax(QKᵀ). They're the same mathematical operation with different constraints. This duality lets Mamba-2 use efficient matrix multiplications (tensor cores) instead of sequential scans, achieving 2-8× speedup over Mamba-1.
